one_line_takeaway: By aligning the watermark green list to the prompt's semantic topic rather than a random partition, TBW gets paraphrase robustness close to heavyweight schemes while keeping KGW-level speed and near-baseline perplexity, and its topic-agnostic max-z-score detector recovers the mark at ~99.6-100% without knowing the topic.
key_finding: TBW matches or beats existing lightweight watermarks on text quality while being substantially more robust to paraphrasing than KGW/SynthID/DiP, at negligible runtime overhead. It cuts perplexity by ~42% over Unigram on OPT-6.7B and ~48% on GEMMA-7B (nearly matching non-watermarked text). Under DIPPER paraphrasing on OPT-6.7B it holds ROC-AUC 0.945 vs KGW 0.826, SynthID 0.650, DiP 0.576 (roughly tied with Unigram's 0.955), and achieves the highest TPR@1%FPR of all schemes. The parameter-free maximum-z-score detector reaches 99.6% (OPT-6.7B) and 100% (GEMMA-7B) detection rates without any topic knowledge, versus ~0.52-0.58 for topic-dependent strict/sliding schemes.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Topic-Based Watermarking (TBW), a lightweight in-generation watermark for LLMs. Instead of randomly partitioning the vocabulary into green/red lists as in KGW, TBW clusters every token into one of K predefined semantic topic lists (e.g. {animals, technology, sports, medicine}) by cosine similarity to topic embeddings. At generation time it extracts the prompt's topic (via KeyBERT), selects the matching topic list as the active 'green list', and adds a bias delta to those tokens' logits, embedding a watermark that is semantically aligned with the content and therefore more resilient to paraphrasing while adding no extra inference passes. The second concrete contribution is a set of three detection schemes (strict topic matching, sliding-window, and a parameter-free maximum-z-score scheme) that recover the watermark without needing to know the generation topic at detection time; the max-z-score variant evaluates all K partitions and takes the largest z-score. NOTE ON RELATED WORK: KGW, Unigram, SynthID-Text, DiPmark, SIR, and EXP/EXP-Edit/ITS-Edit are all prior methods used as comparison baselines, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Standard black-box adversary with access only to LLM outputs, seeking to strip the watermark while preserving semantic meaning. The adversary cannot access model parameters, watermarking keys, or the topic-partition logic; the detection algorithm is public but specific parameters (thresholds, embeddings, and the private random seed used for round-robin distribution of residual tokens) remain private. Three evaluated attack surfaces: (1) text degradation via token-level perturbations (random insertions/deletions/substitutions, plus 'targeted' perturbations focused on content words like nouns/verbs); (2) semantic rephrasing / paraphrasing using external models (PEGASUS, DIPPER); and (3) partition-recovery attacks that try to reconstruct the topic-aligned green lists by exploiting their semantic structure (as demonstrated against KGW-style schemes by Jovanovic et al. 2024). The authors argue TBW's full partition stays unrecoverable without the private residual-token seed.
limitations: Smaller-vocabulary models degrade: OPT-6.7B (50,272 tokens) shows lower detection scores and higher FPR than GEMMA-7B (256,000 tokens) because larger vocabularies yield denser per-topic green lists; degradation is worse at large K (e.g. K=32) on small-vocab models. Max-z-score detection cost scales roughly linearly with K (about 2s at K=4 up to 6.5s at K=32), though detection is offline so it does not affect user-facing latency. Like all list-based z-score watermarks (KGW, SynthID), reliability drops on short text; evaluations use only 200-token generations, and the authors expect worse performance on shorter sequences. Sliding-window detection underperformed strict matching, attributed to the fixed 50-word window; alternative window sizes were left as future work. Under the strongest DIPPER attack absolute performance still drops materially (e.g. TPR@1%FPR on GEMMA-7B falls to 0.196). Dual-use / false-positive risks are acknowledged in an ethics section.

datasets_used: C4 (Raffel et al. 2020) - 100-token prompt, 200 (+/-5) token generation
Models: OPT-6.7B (50,272-token vocab), GEMMA-7B (256,000-token vocab), OPT-2.7B (efficiency)
LLAMA-3.1-8B used as independent 'oracle' model for perplexity scoring
all-MiniLM-L6-v2 for token-topic embedding; KeyBERT for topic extraction
quantitative_results: Perplexity: TBW improves ~42% over Unigram on OPT-6.7B and ~48% on GEMMA-7B
Max-z-score detection rate: 99.6% (OPT-6.7B), 100% (GEMMA-7B); mean z 15.88+/-3.03 and 15.92+/-1.39
Topic-dependent detection rates far lower: Strict Embedding 0.574 (OPT) / 0.584 (GEMMA); Sliding K-means as low as 0.516
DIPPER paraphrasing ROC-AUC on OPT-6.7B: TBW 0.945, Unigram 0.955, SIR 0.891, KGW 0.826, SynthID 0.650, DiP 0.576
PEGASUS ROC-AUC on OPT-6.7B: TBW 0.990, Unigram 0.987, KGW 0.975, SIR 0.971, SynthID 0.910, DiP 0.824
TPR@1%FPR under DIPPER on OPT-6.7B: TBW 0.536 (best), Unigram 0.516, SIR 0.248, KGW 0.124, SynthID 0.058, DiP 0.028
TPR@1%FPR under DIPPER on GEMMA-7B drops to 0.196 (TBW), showing residual degradation under strongest attack
Detection thresholds used: TBW z=4.75, KGW z=4.0, Unigram z=4.0, DiP 1.513, SynthID 0.52, SIR 0.2
Topic scalability on GEMMA-7B: mean max-z drops from ~11 (K=4) to ~7 (K=32); BERTScore F1 stays flat
baselines_compared: PostMark (Chang et al. 2024) and k-SemStamp (Hou et al. 2024b), compared separately as post-processing methods
